markdown:
<definition term="International Trade">The **exchange** of goods and services between **two** **or** **more** **countries**.</definition>

International trade brings in a number of benefits. However, there are even greater benefits if the trade is **free.**

<definition term="Free Trade">An **absence** of **government** **intervention** in **international** **trade**, resulting in **no** imposing **restrictions** of any kind on **imports** and **exports**.</definition>

## Benefits of International Trade


<callout type="tip">Remember, each of the benefits of international trade are interlinked with each other!</callout>

<callout type="note">It is important to understand that many of the **benefits of free trade** arise due to **differences** **in** the **prices** of goods and services **between** **countries**. These differences occur because:

1. Differences in the **access** to **natural** **resources**.
2. Differences in **technology** and **capital**.
3. Differences in **quality** and **pay** of **labour**.</callout>

### Increased competition


**International trade** exposes domestic firms to global competition.

1. The competition pushes **domestic** **producers** to **improve the quality of their products**, while also trying to **minimise their costs of production** in order to stay **competitive** against **foreign firms** and their respective prices. 
2. This leads to domestic firms to becoming **more efficient** and **reducing prices for consumers**.

### Lower prices for consumers


1. International trade allows foreign companies to enter the **markets** at **lower** **prices**. Thereby, consumers are able to buy less expensive products. 
2. Further, as **international trade** leads to **increased competition **and **increased efficiency **for domestic firms, this will also lead to a **reduction** in prices of domestic products for consumers.

### Greater choice


1. With international trade, countries can **import** various goods and services from other countries.
2. This enables consumers to have a **greater choice of products**.

### Acquisition of resources


1. Countries can **import** **natural resources **(timber or oil) or **capital goods** (machinery) that are** unavailable domestically.**
2. This allows them to **expand **their **production capabilities**.

### More foreign exchange earnings


1. When exporting goods, countries acquire **foreign currencies** which can be utilised to **pay for imports** from **other countries**.
2. It can also be used to make **payments abroad **(we will explore this concept in subtopic 4.6)

### Access to larger markets


1. Domestic firms can** grow** and **expand their production** by selling products in **international markets** if the output of the firm is **limited** by the **size** of the domestic market. 
2. This leads to **larger sales **and a** larger consumer base** for the firm. 

### Economies of scale


1. Increasing the **market size** for a firm from **domestic** **markets** to domestic and international markets causes the **demand** to **increase**.
2. This causes to the production level and size of production to i**ncrease as well**, which leads to a **fall in average costs** and provides **economies of scale** to the firms.
3. This allows the firms to **reduce their prices for consumers** too.

### More efficient resource allocation


1. When **free trade **takes place, each country **specialises** in producing the goods and services it can produce **more efficiently with a lower cost of production** than other countries.
2. This reduces the waste of scarce resources and thereby **more efficient resource allocation**.

### More efficient production


1. The competition from foreign firms **forces **domestic firms to **improve their quality of the product** and **reduce costs of production** in order to **retain its revenue **and **supply **to the market. 
2. This leads to a **more efficient production** from **domestic firms**.

## Free Trade


* Under free trade, the **prices** of goods and services that are **imported** and **exported **are determined **solely** by the **market** **forces** of **demand** and **supply**.  

The scenario below show the different trade scenarios for the sale of cloth.

### Diagram Calculations


#### Key notations:


1. The** x-axis** denotes the **quantity** of the good, labelled as **Q**
2. The **y-axis **denotes the **price** of the good, labelled as **P**
3. **Sd **and** Dd **refers to the **domestic supply** and **domestic demand **at the country of choice respectively
4. **Qd** refers to the overall **quantity demanded** and **Qs** refers to the overall **quantity supplied** of the good
5. **Pd** refers to the **domestic price** of the mentioned good at the respective country
6. **Pw **refers to the** world price** and **world supply curve** for the mentioned good 

#### World market price:


![Figure 1. World market price.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/20cb396c7b7527d4d2eaba96ba603ff95ce2c0f3-799x669.png)

1. In **Figure 1, **the **world** **price** **Pw **for **cloth** in this scenario is dictated by the **world demand** and **world supply** of cloth.
2. The** world demand **for cloth is the sum of each countries demands and the **world supply** for cloth is the sum of each countries supplies.
3. Every country that participates in this free trade buys and sells cloth at this **world price** **Pw**. 

<callout type="note">The **world price **is **perfectly elastic **indicates that any country participating in the free trade will buy and sell their respective goods at the **world price **and no other price.</callout>

#### Exports under free trade:


![Figure 2. Exports under free trade.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b54834e8e5a948b92905188c48ee5a76d19c504d-811x674.png)

1. As observed in **Figure 2, **when under free trade, the country **Tradeland** will export a good, in this case **cloth**, only when the domestic prices **Pd** are** lower** than the world price **Pw.**
2. If **Tradeland** decides to export cloth to other countries, the domestic price is not relevant anymore and the price of cloth will be **Pw** both domestically and internationally
3. Due to the increase in price from **Pd** to **Pw**, less number of domestic consumers demand cloth at **Qd** **(Law of Demand) **and a higher quantity of cloth is supplied by the producers at **Qs (Law of Supply)**.
4. Therefore, the producers of cloth at **Tradeland** sells **Qd **quantity of cloth to **domestic consumers** and the excess supply of **Qs-Qd **is **exported** to **other countries** and sold. 
5. The quantity of exports can be determined by **Qs-Qd **and the export revenue earned from this can be calculated by doing: $$(Qs-Qd)*Pw$$

#### Imports under free trade:


![Figure 3. Imports under free trade.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5b1a80a83a17be1de301af29ccfe45e2e43053e2-814x674.png)

1. As observed in **Figure 3, **when under free trade, the country **Tradeland** will import a good, in this case **cloth**, only when the domestic prices **Pd** are** higher** than the world price **Pw.**
2. If **Tradesoles** decides to import **cloth** from other countries, the domestic price is not relevant anymore and the price of **cloth** will be **Pw** both domestically and internationally.
3. Due to the decrease in price from **Pd** to **Pw**, more number of domestic consumers demand **cloth** at **Qd** **(Law of Demand) **and a lower quantity of **cloth** is supplied by the producers at **Qs (Law of Supply)**.
4. Therefore, the producers of **cloth** at **Tradeland** sells **Qs **quantity of cloth to **domestic consumers** and the excess demand of **Qd-Qs **is **imported** from **other countries** and sold. 
5. The **quantity** of **imports** can be determined by **Qd-Qs **and the **import** **expenditure** from this can be calculated by doing: $$(Qd-Qs)*Pw$$
